channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Integrated memristor for mitigating reverse-bias in perovskite solar cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10275-3

A memristor integrated into perovskite photovoltaics aims to mitigate the issues seen with reverse-bias degradation under partial shading.
item.dc:title: Integrated memristor for mitigating reverse-bias in perovskite solar cells
item.dc:creator: Mahdi Mohammadi
Fuxiang Ji
Tristan Sachsenweger
Kazem Meraji
Sharun Parayil Shaji
Wolfgang Tress

item.title: Bistable superlattice switching in a quantum spin Hall insulator
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10309-w

Bistable superlattice switching between two lattice configurations with sharply contrasting periodicities has been observed in monolayer TaIrTe4, a dual quantum spin Hall insulator.
item.dc:title: Bistable superlattice switching in a quantum spin Hall insulator
item.dc:creator: Jian Tang
Thomas Siyuan Ding
Shuhan Ding
Jiangxu Li
Changjiang Yi
Tianxing Tang
Zumeng Huang
Xuehao Wu
Zhiheng Huang
Birender Singh
Tiema Qian

Anyuan Gao
Nikolai Peshcherenko
Zhe Sun
Mohamed Shehabeldin
Kenji Watanabe
Takashi Taniguchi
Abhay N. Pasupathy
Claudia Felser
Kenneth S. Burch
Ni Ni
Yao Wang
Yang Zhang
Su-Yang Xu
Qiong Ma

item.title: Observation of self-bound droplets of ultracold dipolar molecules
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10245-9

Self-bound droplets and droplet arrays are observed in an ultracold gas of strongly dipolar sodium–caesium molecules, establishing ultracold molecules as a system for the exploration of strongly dipolar quantum matter.
item.dc:title: Observation of self-bound droplets of ultracold dipolar molecules
item.dc:creator: Siwei Zhang
Weijun Yuan
Niccolò Bigagli
Haneul Kwak
Tijs Karman
Ian Stevenson
Sebastian Will

item.title: Adventitious carbon breaks symmetry in oxide contact electrification
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10088-w

By measuring charge exchange in a sphere/plate pair composed of identical amorphous silicon dioxide and controlling charging polarity using baking or plasma treatment, adventitious carbon is shown to break symmetry in oxide contact electrification.
item.dc:title: Adventitious carbon breaks symmetry in oxide contact electrification
item.dc:creator: Galien Grosjean
Markus Ostermann
Markus Sauer
Michael Hahn
Christian M. Pichler
Florian Fahrnberger
Felix Pertl
Daniel M. Balazs
Mason M. Link
Seong H. Kim
Devin L. Schrader
Adriana Blanco
Francisco Gracia
Nicolás Mujica
Scott R. Waitukaitis

item.title: Hair-raising: how carbon contamination can drive static charging
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00631-8

Objects made from the same insulator can sometimes acquire different charges when rubbed together, owing to a thin layer of carbon-based material on the object’s surface.
item.dc:title: Hair-raising: how carbon contamination can drive static charging
item.dc:creator: Simone Ciampi

item.title: Leading the charge to explain static electricity
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00839-8

There’s a shocking lack of understanding of the physics underlying this commonplace phenomenon, but researchers are on the case.
item.dc:title: Leading the charge to explain static electricity

item.title: Static electricity is a big mystery — a jolt of fresh research could help to solve it
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 18 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00837-w

The familiar phenomenon has puzzled researchers for centuries, but experiments are finally making sense of its unruly behaviours.
item.dc:title: Static electricity is a big mystery — a jolt of fresh research could help to solve it
item.dc:creator: Jenna Ahart

item.title: Planar Li deposition and dissolution enable practical anode-free pouch cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10402-0

Planar Li deposition and dissolution enable practical anode-free pouch cells
item.dc:title: Planar Li deposition and dissolution enable practical anode-free pouch cells
item.dc:creator: Lei Liu
Yuxuan Xiang
Xingyu Lu
Jianhui Wang

item.title: Molecular basis of oocyte cytoplasmic lattice assembly
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10360-7

Molecular basis of oocyte cytoplasmic lattice assembly
item.dc:title: Molecular basis of oocyte cytoplasmic lattice assembly
item.dc:creator: Shuxian Liu

item.title: Triple-junction solar cells with improved carrier and photon management
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10385-y

Triple-junction solar cells with improved carrier and photon management
item.dc:title: Triple-junction solar cells with improved carrier and photon management

item.title: Polymers with purpose: molecules can squirm free of the pack
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00767-7

Tightly packed molecular chains can start to wiggle in many directions when an enzyme introduces energy.
item.dc:title: Polymers with purpose: molecules can squirm free of the pack
